cataloging_subject.label: Arthur
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse chronicle conventionally titled Arthur: a complete account of Arthur's life and reign preserved uniquely as an interpolation in the Red Book of Bath. The intellectual work is distinct from the surrounding Latin Brut, its sole manuscript witness, Furnivall's editions, later critical editions, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and all photographic reproductions.

catalog_summary.title: Arthur
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably composed 1350-1400; uniquely attested in a manuscript made at Bath c. 1412-1430; first published 1864
catalog_summary.region: England; South-Western Middle English, with the sole witness associated with Bath, Somerset

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English

assertion.notes: Bryan confirms that the poem is untitled in the manuscript and that Furnivall supplied this conventional title.
A current catalog description rather than a manuscript title.
Recorded as the closing formula, not treated as a title supplied by the manuscript.

agents.name: Frederick J. Furnivall
notes: Editor and transcriber of the 1864 first edition and its 1869 second edition, the immediate printed source of CME00055.

assertion.notes: No evidence securely identifies the poet, and the person who inserted or copied the poem into the Latin chronicle need not have composed it.

scope.description: The 1864/1869 source edition rather than authorship of the medieval work
Transcription of the unique manuscript witness for the source edition

assertion.notes: This is the commonly accepted probable range, not a dated authorial copy.

date_statements.display: Uniquely attested in the Red Book of Bath, made c. 1412-1430
notes: The witness date is not silently converted into a composition date.

scope.description: Language of the sole surviving copy

assertion.notes: A witness-language classification does not by itself prove the poet's exact place of composition.

scope.description: Production of the sole manuscript witness

notes: Bath is the manuscript's production place, not a confirmed place of composition for the poem.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Brief prayers, Arthur's epitaph, closing formula, and manuscript marginalia

notes: Some Latin belongs to manuscript marginal annotation or Furnivall's apparatus rather than the continuous Middle English verse.

form_statements.description: A 642-line poem in Furnivall's source-edition numbering, predominantly in rhyming four-stress couplets, with occasional alternate-rhyme passages and metrical variation.

assertion.notes: Useful for discovery, but the work's chronicle form and Brut context make a single unqualified romance classification too narrow.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
The fall and death of King Arthur

scope.description: Mordred's betrayal, the final battle, Arthur's death, and Glastonbury burial
Narratorial invocations and the concluding Pater and Ave request

content_parts.label: Arthur
content_parts.summary: A continuous narrative from Arthur's parentage, coronation, Round Table, continental conquests and conflict with Rome through Mordred's betrayal, Arthur's last battle, death, and burial at Glastonbury, ending by directing readers to the French book for more.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; 642 L elements plus one separately encoded, unnumbered Pater noster / Aue Q line

notes: No formal internal sections are encoded; narrative episodes in the summary are not promoted to manuscript divisions.
The Latin Brut resumes on fol. 46v and is outside this work boundary.

extent.description: Furnivall's source edition numbers the continuous poem through line 642, exactly matching the XML's 642 L elements. Between source-edition lines 632 and 633, the XML separately encodes the single unnumbered display 'Pater noster / Aue /' as Q; Bryan's later critical edition splits and counts this prayer material, so his ending is renumbered through 644. The XML also contains one post-poem editorial commentary paragraph outside the work. The poem occupies manuscript fols. 42v-46r and Furnivall printed pp. 1-19.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse chronicle recounting Arthur's life from conception and coronation through the Round Table, conquests in Britain and France, war against the Roman emperor Lucius, and the return to Mordred's rebellion. Arthur defeats Mordred but is mortally wounded, dies at Glastonbury, and is buried there; the poem closes with prayer and directions to a French book and a continuation about later kings.

Arthur is interpolated between two portions of a Latin Brut in the Red Book of Bath. The surrounding chronicle and the manuscript's many civic, legal, medical, devotional, and historical texts are context for the witness but are not contents of CME00055.

related_works.title: Anglo-Norman Prose Brut, Oldest Version
notes: Kooper and Marvin identify the Oldest Version as the poem's most important source. This records an intellectual source tradition, not a claim that a particular surviving codex was the poet's physical exemplar.
The preferred title is conventional and editor-supplied; the manuscript is untitled.
Work composition, physical witness production, editorial publication, digital encoding, and photographic reproduction are kept as separate layers.
The full 157-canvas manuscript surrogate is bound in the lineage manifest, while this record maps only the poem's exact folio and canvas range.
The source claim is updated from Furnivall's broad Geoffrey-of-Monmouth account to Kooper and Marvin's evidence for the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition date and place be narrowed beyond the probable 1350-1400 range and the South-Western language of its sole copy?
next_steps: Consult current dialect and textual scholarship without treating Bath manuscript production as proof of authorship there.
Compare linguistic strata against the now-public full manuscript images.

open_questions.question: Which copy or textual branch of the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut most closely represents the poem's immediate French source?
next_steps: Consult the complete Kooper-Marvin comparison and collate its diagnostic readings against the manuscript.
Do not equate a comparison codex with the lost historical exemplar without evidence.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00055.xml.
evidence.summary: Establishes the encoded title page, poem text, incipit and explicit, Middle English and embedded Latin, folio/page/column milestones, marginal notes, editorial typography, and the source-edition work boundary.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of General Corpus CME00055.xml.
evidence.summary: The XML has three BODY-level DIV1 elements, 642 L elements, one separately encoded unnumbered Q prayer line, 127 NOTE, 21 PB, 190 MILESTONE, and one post-poem commentary paragraph. Direct comparison with Furnivall printed page 19 confirms that its 642 L elements match the source edition's numbering through line 642.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Arthur: A Short Sketch of His Life and History in English Verse of the First Half of the Fifteenth Century, 2nd ed., EETS OS 2 (London: Trübner, 1869; title-page imprint 1864).
evidence.locator: bound-volume PDF pages 8-34; poem at physical PDF pages 14-32, printed pages 1-19
evidence.summary: Primary source-edition evidence for Furnivall's supplied title, direct manuscript transcription, 1869 revision statement, poem text, lineation, and editorial handling of contractions, punctuation, and marginal notes.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 89789, The Red Book of Bath.
evidence.locator: scope and contents; physical characteristics; custodial history
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, former Longleat House MS 55 designation, 1412-1430 Bath production, material and binding, unique Arthur at fols. 42v-46r, surrounding Latin Brut, public digitised-content link, physical access restrictions, and April 2025 acquisition.

evidence.citation: British Library IIIF Presentation manifest for Add MS 89789.
evidence.locator: complete object, 157 canvases; Arthur at canvases 95-102, fols. 42v-46r
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete physical-object sequence and exact poem boundaries. Visual inspection shows two-column verse with red line fillers and marginal annotations; fol. 46v resumes the decorated Latin chronicle.

evidence.citation: Mildred Willingham Bryan, A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur (PhD diss., University of Alabama, 1977).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. 1-33; critical text pp. 38-66; verse discussion pp. 23-29
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship, untitled-manuscript status, the commonly cited 1350-1400 range, South-Western dialect, predominantly iambic-tetrameter rhyming couplets with variation, and a complete critical text and verse translation. Bryan's later critical text numbers through 644 because its treatment of the Pater/Ave prayer differs from Furnivall's 642-line source edition.

evidence.citation: Marije Pots and Erik Kooper, 'Arthur: A New Critical Edition of the Fifteenth-Century Middle English Verse Chronicle,' The Medieval Chronicle 7 (2011): 239-266.
evidence.locator: public preview pp. 239-240; full article pp. 239-266
evidence.summary: Modern critical-edition evidence for the work's verse-chronicle classification, probable later-fourteenth-century composition, sole witness, and editorial history.

evidence.citation: Erik Kooper and Julia Marvin, 'A Source for the Middle English Poem Arthur,' Arthuriana 22.4 (2012/2013): 25-45.
evidence.summary: Identifies the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut as the poem's most important source. The issue is Winter 2012, while Utrecht's institutional record dates publication to 2013.

evidence.citation: Erik Kooper, 'Longleat House MS 55: An Unacknowledged Brut Manuscript?' in The Prose Brut and Other Late Medieval Chronicles (York Medieval Press, 2016).
evidence.summary: Places Arthur precisely inside the surrounding Latin Brut, confirms the poem's source relationship to the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut, and reviews dialect, manuscript context, and editorial history.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00055 and collection description.
evidence.summary: Confirms the CME identifier and collection-level keyed-text/proofreading context. The item route presented a Cloudflare challenge during this review, so exact text details were checked against the pinned XML and source edition.
